settings.heading:
Your Gut Feeds
Your Brain.
settings.subheading: Your gut and brain communicate constantly. What you snack on shapes your energy, focus, and cravings.

settings.title: Afternoon Energy Crashes
settings.body: A daily 2–4pm slump often means unstable blood sugar and an under-fuelled gut-brain axis. Your brain needs steady energy, not sugar spikes.

settings.title: Stress-Driven Cravings
settings.body: When cortisol rises, your gut sends urgent hunger signals. If you snack when stressed — not hungry — your gut-brain axis may be stuck in a cortisol loop.

settings.title: Bloating After Meals
settings.body: Frequent bloating can signal microbiome imbalance. When gut diversity drops, digestion slows and gut-brain communication becomes noisy.

settings.title: Brain Fog or Mood Dips
settings.body: About 95% of serotonin is produced in the gut. When the microbiome is off, mood, clarity, and emotional balance can suffer.

settings.title: Never Feeling Satisfied
settings.body: If hunger returns soon after eating, your satiety hormones may not be signalling properly — a common gut-brain communication gap.

settings.title: Disrupted Sleep or Recovery
settings.body: The gut-brain axis regulates melatonin and sleep signaling. Microbiome imbalance can quietly affect sleep and recovery.

settings.heading:
Signs Your Gut-Brain Axis
Needs Support
settings.sub: These aren't random bad days. Your body is sending signals that the gut-brain axis is out of balance.
settings.callout_text: Do any of these sound familiar? They're often signals your gut-brain axis needs support — and the right snacks can help restore balance.

settings.diagram_caption: Signals travel in both directions — 24 hours a day
settings.heading:
Your Gut and Brain
Are Always Talking
settings.body: The gut brain connection is one of the most important — and most overlooked — systems in your body. Your gut and brain are linked by a powerful two-way network called the gut-brain axis: a system of nerves, hormones, and neurotransmitters that runs between your digestive system and your brain. Scientists call the primary pathway the vagus nerve.
settings.callout: When the gut is inflamed or out of balance, the brain feels it — and vice versa. This is why snacking isn't just about calories. It's about communication.

settings.label: Microbes in the gut
settings.sub: Bacteria, viruses, and fungi that shape your health

settings.label: Of serotonin made in gut
settings.sub: The mood neurotransmitter is primarily gut-produced

settings.label: Microbial species
settings.sub: Diversity is linked to better health outcomes

settings.label: Of immune cells in gut
settings.sub: Your digestive system is your largest immune organ

settings.text: Influence mood, cognition, and mental clarity
Signal hunger, fullness, and food cravings

settings.heading: Why the Microbiome Matters
settings.body_intro: Your gut microbiome health depends on what you eat more than almost any other factor. Your gut is home to trillions of beneficial microbes — bacteria, viruses, and fungi — that act as a second command centre for your body. Foods that support the microbiome build diversity; ultra-processed foods deplete it.
settings.closing_body: Every snack is a vote for or against that balance.

settings.title: Feed the Gut, Signal the Brain
settings.body: Prebiotic fiber feeds beneficial gut bacteria. A strong microbiome produces more serotonin, reduces inflammation, and sends clear signals to the brain — for better mood and focus.

settings.title: Calm the Cortisol Loop
settings.body: Adaptogens help regulate cortisol — the stress hormone that drives cravings and reactive eating. When cortisol stabilizes, gut-brain stress signals quiet down and energy becomes more balanced.

settings.title: Trigger Real Satiety Signals
settings.body: Protein and fiber activate gut hormones that signal fullness to the brain. Together they stabilize blood sugar and help prevent cravings between meals.

settings.title: Protect with Antioxidants
settings.body: Antioxidant-rich whole foods reduce oxidative stress along the gut-brain axis. This helps protect the gut lining and supports healthy communication between gut and brain.

settings.label: Sugar Alcohols
settings.body: Erythritol and maltitol are common gut irritants. We use none. Sweetness from monk fruit only.

settings.label: Palm Oil
settings.body: Linked to inflammation and environmental harm. Our fats come from almond butter, MCT oil, and flaxseed.

settings.label: Added Sugar
settings.body: Zero added sugar means no glucose spike, no insulin crash, and no disruption to your gut microbiome.

settings.label: Artificial Anything
settings.body: No artificial sweeteners, colors, or flavors. Your microbiome struggles with ultra-processed ingredients — ours doesn't use them.
